coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with strong excitation-dependent fluorescence changes towards pH. Application as nanosensors for a broad range of pH
coredata.dc:creator: Barati, Ali
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Abdollahi, Hamid
coredata.dc:description: In this study, preparation of novel pH-sensitive N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using glucose and urea is reported. The prepared NCDs present strong excitation-dependent fluorescence changes towards the pH that is a new behavior from these nanomaterials. By taking advantage of this unique behavior, two separated ratiometric pH sensors using emission spectra of the NCDs for both acidic (pH 2.0 to 8.0) and basic (pH 7.0 to 14.0) ranges of pH are constructed. Additionally, by considering the entire Excitation–Emission Matrix (EEM) of NCDs as analytical signal and using a suitable multivariate calibration method, a broad range of pH from 2.0 to 14.0 was well calibrated. The multivariate calibration method was independent from the concentration of NCDs and resulted in a very low average prediction error of 0.067 pH units. No changes in the predicted pH under UV irradiation (for 3 h) and at high ionic strength (up to 2 M NaCl) indicated the high stability of this pH nanosensor. The practicality of this pH nanosensor for pH determination in real water samples was validated with good accuracy and repeatability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
pH sensing
Ratiometric sensors
Multivariate calibration
Fluorescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council for the financial support of this work. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image, b) full scan XPS spectrum, c) XPS high-resolution scan of the C1s region and d) FTIR spectrum of prepared NCDs.
The emission spectra of NCDs excited at various wavelengths from 270 to 400 nm at pH 2.0, 7.0 and 14.0.
The emission spectra of NCDs at different pH values excited at 280 (a) and 340 nm (b), and their related changes in the fluorescence intensity and emission maximum.

Dual excitation mode emission spectra of NCDswith excitation wavelengths 270 and 380 nm (a), and 290 and 350 nm (c), as a function of pH, and their related ratiometric pH calibration plots (b and d). The emission spectra are normalized to the maximum intensity of the emission spectra excited at 270 nm and 290 nm, respectively.

Three dimension score plot of the first three principal component of the augmented EEM data sets at different pH values of 2, 3, 4, 5, 5.5, 6, 6.2, 6.4, 6.6, 6.8, 7, 7.2, 7.4, 7.6, 7.8, 8, 8.5, 9, 9.5, 10, 10.5, 11, 11.5, 12, 12.5, 13, and 14 (from left to right).
Predicted pH values versus their actual values for the calibration set (unfilled circles) and test set (filled circles) using BPANN (a) and PLS (b).

a) pH reversibility study of the NCDs between pH 4, 7 and 10 using the EEM data sets and BPANN model. pH stability of under NCDs b) UV irradiation c) ionic strength, and d) presence of metal cations (by adding 5 μM of each ion to the blank solution, in Britton-Robinson buffer 0.02 M, pH = 7.0).
The actual pH values of calibration and test sets and their predicted values using BPANN and PLS.
Comparison of different recently published fluorescent sensors for the determination of pH.
Determination of pH in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with strong excitation-dependent fluorescence changes towards pH. Application as nanosensors for a broad range of pH

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences, Zanjan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, preparation of novel pH-sensitive N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using glucose and urea is reported. The prepared NCDs present strong excitation-dependent fluorescence changes towards the pH that is a new behavior from these nanomaterials. By taking advantage of this unique behavior, two separated ratiometric pH sensors using emission spectra of the NCDs for both acidic (pH 2.0 to 8.0) and basic (pH 7.0 to 14.0) ranges of pH are constructed. Additionally, by considering the entire Excitation–Emission Matrix (EEM) of NCDs as analytical signal and using a suitable multivariate calibration method, a broad range of pH from 2.0 to 14.0 was well calibrated. The multivariate calibration method was independent from the concentration of NCDs and resulted in a very low average prediction error of 0.067 pH units. No changes in the predicted pH under UV irradiation (for 3 h) and at high ionic strength (up to 2 M NaCl) indicated the high stability of this pH nanosensor. The practicality of this pH nanosensor for pH determination in real water samples was validated with good accuracy and repeatability.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, there is a growing interest to develop nanomaterials with sensing capabilities in various fields like diagnostics, drug delivery and environmental monitoring. Accordingly, a variety of nanomaterials with various sensing strategies has been modified to achieve these goals [1–4]. In this regard, considering the important role of pH in a broad range of applications from environmental to industrial to biomedical systems, construction of fluorescence-based pH nanosensors has received a great attention. Thus, several pH nanosensors based on single intensity responses [5–7] and many ratiometric nanosensors have been established for pH measurements [8–14]. The ratiometric sensors, as an example of robust tools, provide an internal standard and avoid many interfering potentials, as compared to the single intensity based sensors. However, most of the reported ratiometric fluorescence-based pH nanosensors relay on pH-sensitive fluorescence dyes conjugated on the nanomaterials surface or embedded in their structure. As expected, such nanosensors generally suffer from photobleaching and respond in a sigmoidal manner to changes in pH, allowing a limited dynamic range to ca. pKa ± 1.5. Currently, a significant focus in this area is directed towards novel pH sensing materials, especially from low-cost materials, to develop high-performance sensors for a broad range of pH with improved sensitivity, stability, and selectivity [15,16].

Carbon dots (CDs) as a new class of photoluminescent carbonaceous nanomaterials have attracted considerable attention in recent years, owing to their several favorable characteristics including water solubility, biocompatibility, high quantum yield, as well as excellent optical and chemical stabilities [17–20]. These superior advantages make these nanomaterials as prime candidates for a variety of potential applications in optoelectronic devices [21], biological labeling, bio-imaging [22] and sensing [23].

Thanks to the presence of multiple reactive oxygenated groups like hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, the photoluminescence properties of CDs are usually pH dependent [24–28]. Up to now, several CDs or N-doped CDs (NCDs) have been directly used as pH sensors [29–32]. As example, Huang et al. [29] synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through a one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid and dicyandiamide, which were pH-sensitive between 1.81 and 8.96. The prepared GQDs were evaluated as pH sensors with applications from real water to intracellular contents. Qian et al. [30] reported N-CDs with pH sensitivity between 5.0 and 13.0. Wang et al. [31] reported pH-sensitive carbon nanoparticles by the carbonization of polyethylenimine and reported them as fluorescent pH nanosensors at pH values between 2.0 and 12.0. However, these reported pH sensors were based on single fluorescence intensity response that suffer from interfering potentials especially in complex media.

To overcome this limitation, some novel CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence pH sensors have been developed [33–36]. Liu et al. developed the first ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on CDs without incorporation of organic dyes or hybridization of semiconductor quantum dots, and reported the multiple sensing capacities of these CDs including the pH sensing on the basis of ratiometric fluorescence [33]. However, this ratiometric fluorescence could be only used to calibrate the high acidic or high basic range of pH. Additionally, other reported CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence pH sensors are based on covalently linking pH-sensitive organic dyes on the surface of CDs [34–36] that despite the different fabrication strategies, response to a limited range of pH. Concerning the unique chemical and physical advantages of CDs, as compared with fluorescent dyes or other nanomaterials, it is surely obvious that there is plenty of room to improve the performance of these nanomaterials as novel pH-sensitive nanosensors.

In this study, we report a facile one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize NCDs using glucose and urea to construct a concentration-independent pH nanosensor with potential application in a broad range of pH. Interestingly, the synthesized NCDs present a strong excitation-dependent fluorescence change towards the pH. It means that emission spectra recorded at different excitation wavelengths present different spectral change behaviors towards the pH. Using this property, it was possible to employ NCDs as ratiometric pH sensors in acidic or basic pH ranges. Additionally, for the first time, we show how recording the excitation–emission matrix (EEM) of NCDs at different pH values accompanied by an appropriate multivariate calibration method, to simultaneously consider all spectral changes, can successfully assist to present the synthesized NCDs as a robust pH-sensor in a broad pH range from 2.0 to 14.0.

All analytical grade reagents were of the highest purity available and used as received. Reagent grade glucose and urea were purchased from Merck (Germany). Britton-Robinson (BR) aqueous universal buffer solutions (0.02 M Boric acid, 0.02 M Glacial acetic acid and 0.02 M phosphoric acid) were used in experiments and the pH values were adjusted with concentrated NaOH and HCl.

A Metrohm pH Meter 713 was used to read the pH values. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of NCDs were recorded over the range from 500 to 4000 cm−1 on a Bruker infrared spectrophotometer. UV–vis spectra were obtained with an Agilent 8453 Diode Array Spectrophotometer, and fluorescence studies were carried out using a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Zeiss EM10C microscope. The XPS analysis was performed on a X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, 8025-BesTec twin anode XR3E2 X-ray source system).

A one-step hydrothermal treatment was used to prepare NCDs as follows. 2 g glucose and 1 g urea were dissolved in 20 mL double distilled water, and then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 12 h. After this time, the black carbonized solution was cooled to room temperature. The solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.2 μm membrane to further remove large particles and then was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO¼ 0.5–1.0 and 3.5–5 kD, Float-A-LyzerG2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) for 3 days and finally were dried at 70 °C.

The typical fluorescence data recording was carried out as follows. A desired volume of NCDs from its stock solution was diluted to Britton-Robinson (BR) aqueous universal buffer solutions (0.02 M). After adjusting the pH value, 3 mL of the solution was transferred to the quartz cell of spectrofluorimeter. The EEM data were recorded at excitation wavelengths from 270 to 400 nm at regular steps of 10 nm and the emission wavelengths from 300 to 650 nm. The excitation and emission monochromator slit widths were 5 nm and a scanning rate of 700 nm/min was used.

The NCDs in this work were prepared using glucose and urea through a one-pot hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 12 h. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1 a) shows that the mean size of the NCDs is 4–6 nm. X-ray photoelectronspectroscopy (XPS) and elementary analysis were performed to determine the composition of the NCDs. The full scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 1b) shows the existence of carbon (C, 1s, 285 eV), nitrogen (N, 1s, 400 eV) and oxygen (O, 1s, 532 eV). The high resolution scan of the C 1s region shows four peaks, indicating that carbon is present in four different chemical environments, corresponding to sp2 C (from CC or CC) at 285.1 eV, sp3 C (from CN and CO) at 286.5 eV, CN at 287.5 eV, and CO at 288.8 eV (Fig. 1c). The O 1s spectrum presents two peaks, which are assigned to CO at 531.4 eV and COH at 532.5 eV (Fig. S1a). In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. S1b) there are two peaks at 399.2 and 400.3 eV, which are attributed to the pyrrole-like N (CNC) and graphitic N or NH bands, respectively [37].

The results of elementary analysis for NCDs in the presence of urea are: C 40.0 wt %, N 15.7 wt %, H 6.2 wt % and O (calculated) 38.1 wt %, while for those in the absence of urea no nitrogen content was detected. The existence of surface functional groups of CDs was studied using FTIR spectra (Fig. 1d). The broad band at 3200–3550 cm−1 is assigned to COH and NH stretching vibrations and peaks appear at 2800–2950 cm−1 are attributed to the CH stretching vibrations. The bending vibrations of the NH groups could appear at 1403 cm−1 and peaks at about 1674 cm−1 indicate the presence of CO stretching vibration. The band at around 1067 cm−1 present the existence of CO from hydroxyl, ester, epoxide or ether groups. These data reveal that the prepared NCDs are functionalized with COOH, –OH, and amine groups, which results in their excellent water solubility without needing to further chemical modification.

The absorption spectrum of NCDs (Fig. S2) shows a strong optical absorption with two distinguish peaks in the UV region (230 and 280 nm), with a tail extending into the visible range. The peak at 230 nm is ascribed to π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds in the carbon core, while the peak at 280 nm can be attributed to n-π* transition, namely, the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states [38]. The XRD pattern of this nanomaterial (Fig. S3) displays a broad peak centered at about 2θ = 20° that can be related to a graphitic nature with highly disordered carbon atoms [39].

The emission spectra of NCDs excited at various wavelengths from 270 to 400 nm (at pH 2.0, 7.0 and 14.0) are shown in Fig. 2 , which present the common excitation-dependence emission behaviors of CDs. The Rayleigh scattering were eliminated from the spectra and then interpolated using the interpolation function in MATLAB (“interp1” function with a cubic equation). At pH 7.0, the strongest fluorescence emission band, located at 450 nm, is observed under excitation of 350 nm and has a fluorescent quantum yield of 18.4%, using quinine sulfate as a reference. It has been suggested that the excitation-dependence emission behaviors of CDs is arisen from the abundant functional groups with various energy levels on the surface of CDs that can form a series of surface state emissive traps as isolated fluorophores [40]. Thus, by illuminating CDs with a given excitation wavelength, a certain surface state will dominate the emission spectrum. This suggestion has been supported by indication of emission spectra consisting of spectral overlapped bands [41,42].

Additionally, it was observed that the fluorescence of NCDs is very sensitive to pH and, despite the intensity of emission spectra, their peak positions also change with change in the pH of the solution. Moreover, this pH-sensitivity is strongly excitation-dependent, so that the emission spectra excited at different excitation wavelength show different intensity and peak position changes. As example, the emission spectra of the NCDs upon excitation at 280 nm and 340 nm as a function of pH are shown in Fig. 3 a and b, respectively. Changes in the intensity and position of the emission maxima are presented separately for more detail. Upon excitation at 270 nm, the intensity of the emission spectrum shows the maximum value at pH 3.0 and generally decrease as pH is increased. In addition, the emission maximum shifts from 404 to 435 nm when pH changes from 2.0 to 9.0, and remain almost constant at the higher pH values. Nevertheless, under excitation at 340 nm, the intensity of the emission spectrum shows a negligible change at pH range from 3.0 to 10.0 but strongly decreases at higher pH values. Besides, the maximum emission of this peak shows a slight shift to shorter wavelengths only at basic pH values that is completely inverse of that observed for the emission spectrum excited at 280 nm.

The comparison of these spectral variations with pH clearly reveals that the changes in emission spectra excited at different excitation wavelengths may be completely different. The pH dependency of the fluorescence of CDs is generally attributed to the reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of CDs [33]. It has been reported that the fluorescent species in the CDs should have basic/acidic sites relevant to the photoluminescence emissions, which are quenched in one media but restored in another media [27,29,31]. As example, CDs prepared by polyethylenimine had the highest fluorescence intensity in acidic media and quenched in basic media [31] while, CDs prepared from pyrolysis of ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid salts represented a reverse behavior [27]. Accordingly, Huang et al. [29] use the relation between the zeta potential and the pH to expressed that the reversible protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups such as amide and carboxylic groups on the surface of GQDs, prepared from citric acid and dicyandiamide, should be responsible for the variation of the fluorescence. The possible proton transfer from the protonated nitrogen to the conjugated carbon structure [30], and effecting the inner charge-transfers and the energy bands [26] with change in pH have been also reported as the possible mechanisms for pH dependency of NCDs. As mentioned, fluorescence of CDs can arise from the abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs with various energy levels or from molecule states related to isolated fluorophores connected on the surface or interior of the carbon backbone [40]. Thus, the pH dependency of prepared NCDs can come from reversible protonation and deprotonation of the various functional groups on the surface of NCDs (each of which possessing a unique pKa) or isolated fluorophores in NCDs with different energy states in acidic and basic media [29,43,44]. However, neither the emission intensity changes nor the emission maxima shifts display an acceptable linear relationship over a broad range of pH.

Generally, compared with the single response fluorescence intensity detection, ratiometric method avoids the influence of any change in concentration or other factors in the test procedure. Thus, considering the different behavior of emission spectra upon different excitation wavelengths, the ratiometric fluorescence detection upon dual excitation mode was used to construct a ratiometric pH sensor. Fig. 4 shows two of the best-obtained possible dual excitation ratiometric calibration plots against the pH for NCDs. This Figure shows the emission spectra of NCDs excited at 270 and 380 nm, and 290 and 350 nm, respectively, and their related ratiometric intensities that can be used for linear estimation of pH in the acidic (from 2.0 to 8.0) and the basic (from 7.0 to 14.0) ranges of pH. It is interesting that using these NCDs and plotting these two ratiometric calibration plots the pH range from 2.0 to 14.0 can be well calibrated. However, as can be seen, none of these ratiometric signals could individually calibrate the whole range of pH and each one is ideal for estimation of pH over a certain range.

We then used the EEM of NCDs as measurement response to calibrate with pH, using a suitable multivariate calibration method. The EEM consists the entire spectral changes including the spectral shifts and intensity ratios and might help to extend the calibration range of pH. Thus, the EEM of NCDs over a pH range of 2.0–14.0 was recorded. In order to eliminate the total fluorescence intensity effects and construct a concentration independent pH nanosensor, each EEM data set was normalized to its maximum value. Then, each normalized EEMdata (nλEx,nλEm) was vectorized to obtain a long vector of dimensions (1, nλEx × nλEm). All vectorized data sets were augmented together to give a new augmented matrix with dimensions of (npH, nλEx × nλEm), where npH is the pH values related to each vectorized data set. Thus, each row of the out coming augmented matrix contained the fluorescence spectra of NCDs solution at a certain pH value recorded at different excitation wavelengths sequentially linked together (Fig. S3).

The augmented matrix was first analyzed with principal component analysis (PCA) [45]. The results are presented as a three-dimensional score plot in Fig. 5 . The first three PCs explained 76.9%, 11.0%, and 5.1% of the total variance, respectively. Interestingly, it can be obviously seen that data sets with different pH values are completely discriminated. Even data sets with 0.2 unit differences in the pH (between 6.0 and 8.0) are completely separated, which shows the highest sensitivity of NCDs at neutral range of pH [24–28]. These results clearly indicated that the EEM of NCDs at different pH values have sufficient information to calibrate the entire pH range.

In order to estimate the accuracy of the regression models, the pH value of 24 data sets (test set) recorded at the pH values not included in the calibration set were predicted using the obtained model (filled circles in Fig. 6a and Table 1). The pH values of this set were predicted with the root mean square error of prediction (RMSEP) of 0.079, which is in good agreement with RMSECV for the calibration set.

Despite the BPANN, the partial least-squares (PLS) regression method [47] as a well-known linear multivariate method was also used to model the calibration set and prediction of the test set. The values of 0.176 and 0.303 for RMSECV and RMSEP with the optimum numbers of latent variables were achieved, respectively (Table 1 and Fig. 6b). Obviously, BPANN has given much better results than the PLS model. This result might be explained by the nonlinearity nature of the spectral changes in the data sets towards the pH.

One of the main problems of chemical pH sensors is their repeatability. The repeatability of pH measurements in this study was investigated by performing several cycles from pH 4.0 to 7.0 to 11.0 and reverting the pH in the same solution of NCDs using concentrated NaOH and HCl. Even after 7 cycles, no drift in the predicted pH values was observed (Fig. 7 a). This experiment demonstrate that the response of NCDs toward the pH is fully repeatable.

Generally, compared with organic fluorescent dyes and other fluorescent nanoparticles, the CDs are known for their fluorescence stability when irradiated with UV-light. Fig. 7b shows that no change in the predicted pH was observed under irradiation of CDs solution at 245 nm up to 3 h Fig. 7c shows the effect of ionic strength on the prediction of pH, which indicate that NCDs are really insensitive to the changes in the ionic strength even up to 2 M NaCl. The effect of common metal cations by adding 5 μM of each ion to the blank solution was investigated and no significant change in predicted pH was observed (Fig. 7d). In Table 2 , we have compared the dynamic pH range of published CDs-based pH sensors and some other recently published fluorescent pH-nanosensors to our report. As can be seen, our sensor not only calibrate the broadest range of pH but also is a concentration-independent sensor without using any additional fluorescent dyes.

Following the fluorescence stability of NCDs at different conditions, determination of pH in tap water, well water and, municipal wastewater (of Kermanshah) samples using NCDs with the multivariate method was investigated. pH of each water sample was predicted by recording the EEM of the added NCDs to the untreated water samples and compared with those obtained by a pH-meter glass electrode. The results (Table 3 ) show good agreement between the values predicted by our proposed method and the pH-meter. These results indicated that the complexity of the sample matrix have no influence on the performance of the proposed method and this method can be utilized as an excellent fluorescent pH indicator for real water samples. It should be noted that our sensor is not exempted from all other fluorescence sensors that absorbing the excitation and/or emission wavelengths by colored real samples (inner filter effect (IFE)) can affect the sensing accuracy. However, even the municipal wastewater sample showed a negligible absorbance at wavelengths higher than 270 nm, indicating no significant IFE in these measurements (Fig. S5).

In conclusion, we have developed high pH-sensitive NCDs from glucose and urea by one-pot hydrothermal method. The fluorescence change of the prepared NCDs toward the pH was strongly excitation-dependent. We used this feature to employing these NCDs as ratiometric and multivariate concentration-independent pH sensors without using any additional fluorescence dyes or nanoparticles. From an analytical point of view, we showed how evaluation from univariate to ratiometric to multivariate calibration, could improve the calibration range to introduce these NCDs as robust and sensitive pH-nanosensors over the pH range of 2.0–14.0. It was shown that ANN has greater potential than PLS to calibrate the EEM data sets to pH. The obtained average prediction error for ANN was 0.067 pH units that is a very low value. Observing no change in the predicted pH under UV irradiation and even at high ionic strengths showed the high stability of this pH nanosensor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council for the financial support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.05.011.
